title: SQL for Absolute Beginners
subtitle: Full course blueprint in modules, sections, topics, exercises, quizzes, and projects
intendedFor: Best for self-learners, tutors, beginner bootcamps, community college intros, and first-time database students.

courseOverview.recommendedSequence: Module 0 through Module 15 in order. The flow moves from database foundations -> reading data -> filtering -> analysis -> joins -> data changes -> schema design -> projects.
courseOverview.summary: The flow moves from database foundations to reading data, filtering, analysis, joins, data changes, schema design, and projects.

moduleSummary.title: Getting Started with SQL and Databases
Working with Text, Lists, Ranges, and NULL
Grouping Data with GROUP BY and HAVING
Real Beginner SQL Projects and Course Capstone

authoringGuidance: Use topics as individual lessons or tutoring sessions.
Use the module purpose to explain the why before syntax.
Use the exercises for guided practice and the project for consolidation.
Keep quizzes short and verbal at first; beginners learn faster when they explain their thinking out loud.

modules.title: Getting Started with SQL and Databases
modules.purpose: Build the learner's mental model before syntax: what SQL is, how databases organize information, and how a question becomes a query.
learningObjectives: Explain what SQL does in plain language.
Identify rows, columns, tables, and simple keys.
Describe how data questions map to database queries.
guidedExercises: Label the parts of a sample table: table name, columns, rows, and key candidate.
Translate three business questions into data questions.
Run a provided starter query inside a SQL editor and describe the result.
quizFocus: Define database, table, row, and column.
Explain why spreadsheets and relational tables are similar but not identical.
Identify when data should live in more than one table.
modules.moduleProject: Create a one-page data map for a tiny school or store database showing 3-4 tables and what each table stores.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Create a one-page data map for a tiny school or store database showing 3-4 tables and what each table stores.

sections.title: What SQL Is
sections.description: Start with the big picture: what SQL is, why people use it, and how it helps answer questions from data.

sections.title: Understanding Tables
sections.description: Learn how tables organize information through rows, columns, records, fields, and clear names.

sections.title: Database Thinking
sections.description: Build the mental model for organizing data, connecting tables, and turning real questions into database questions.

sections.title: First SQL Environment
sections.description: Get comfortable with where SQL runs, how editors work, and how to read the results of a first query.

modules.description: Build the learner's mental model before syntax: what SQL is, how databases organize information, and how a question becomes a query.

modules.title: Your First Queries with SELECT
modules.purpose: Teach learners to retrieve data confidently with SELECT and FROM before filtering or joining tables.
learningObjectives: Write valid SELECT statements against a single table.
Choose between selecting all columns and selecting only needed columns.
Read result sets without confusion.
guidedExercises: Write four SELECT statements using one table and different column combinations.
Predict the output before running each query.
Correct five broken beginner queries.
quizFocus: What is the role of SELECT?
What is the role of FROM?
Why is SELECT * useful for exploration but risky in real applications?
modules.moduleProject: Build a one-page query sheet with ten starter SELECT queries against a student, product, or movie table.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Build a one-page query sheet with ten starter SELECT queries against a student, product, or movie table.

sections.title: Intro to SELECT
sections.description: Learn the purpose of SELECT and how a basic query asks a table for information.

sections.title: Reading Data from a Table
sections.description: Practice choosing useful columns and reading clean results from a single table.

sections.title: SQL Syntax Basics
sections.description: Understand the small syntax details that make SQL readable and valid, including keywords, semicolons, and casing.

sections.title: Practice with Basic Queries
sections.description: Strengthen the basic SELECT pattern by querying one column, multiple columns, and full tables.

modules.description: Teach learners to retrieve data confidently with SELECT and FROM before filtering or joining tables.

modules.title: Filtering Data with WHERE
modules.purpose: Introduce decision-making in queries by teaching learners to keep only the rows they actually need.
learningObjectives: Use WHERE to narrow results correctly.
Compare text and numeric values using SQL operators.
Combine logical conditions without changing the intended meaning.
guidedExercises: Find all rows that match an exact value.
Filter a table using at least one range condition and one text condition.
Rewrite mixed AND/OR logic with parentheses to make it explicit.
quizFocus: What happens if a query has no WHERE clause?
When should you use AND versus OR?
Why do parentheses help when multiple conditions are involved?
modules.moduleProject: Create a 'data detective' worksheet with ten filtering prompts and answer them with SQL.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Create a 'data detective' worksheet with ten filtering prompts and answer them with SQL.

sections.title: Intro to Filtering
sections.description: Learn why filtering matters and how WHERE keeps only the rows that answer the question.

sections.title: Comparison Operators
sections.description: Use comparison operators to match exact values, ranges, and numeric conditions correctly.

sections.title: Filtering with Multiple Conditions
sections.description: Combine conditions with AND, OR, and NOT while keeping the query logic clear.

sections.title: Beginner Filtering Practice

topics.title: Filter by more than one rule

sections.description: Apply filtering skills to text, numbers, and multi-rule questions while avoiding common mistakes.
bullets: Filter by more than one rule

modules.description: Introduce decision-making in queries by teaching learners to keep only the rows they actually need.

modules.title: Sorting and Limiting Results
modules.purpose: Help beginners control output so answers are easier to read, rank, and compare.
learningObjectives: Sort result sets in meaningful ways.
Use LIMIT to keep only the most relevant rows.
Combine sorting and limiting to answer ranking questions.
guidedExercises: Show the top 5 most expensive products.
Show the 10 most recent rows in a table with dates.
Sort by two columns and explain how ties are handled.
quizFocus: What is the default sort direction in most SQL engines?
Why is LIMIT often paired with ORDER BY?
What does a secondary sort do?
modules.moduleProject: Build a 'top performers' report for a sample sales or grades dataset.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Build a 'top performers' report for a sample sales or grades dataset.

sections.title: Sorting Data
sections.description: Learn how ORDER BY changes the order of results so the most useful rows are easier to see.

sections.title: Sorting by Multiple Columns
sections.description: Practice sorting by more than one column and understand how SQL handles ties.

sections.title: Limiting Output
sections.description: Use LIMIT with sorting to focus on the top, bottom, newest, or most relevant rows.

sections.title: Practice with Output Control
sections.description: Build clearer result sets by combining sorting, limiting, and careful result reading.

modules.description: Help beginners control output so answers are easier to read, rank, and compare.

modules.title: Working with Text, Lists, Ranges, and NULL
modules.purpose: Expand filtering to real-world cases like partial text, multiple choices, ranges, and missing values.
learningObjectives: Match partial strings with LIKE.
Filter using lists and ranges with IN and BETWEEN.
Handle NULL safely using IS NULL and IS NOT NULL.
guidedExercises: Find names that start with a given letter.
Find rows whose category is in a list of allowed values.
Identify rows with missing contact information.
quizFocus: Why does = NULL not work the way beginners expect?
What is the difference between % and _ in LIKE patterns?
When is IN cleaner than multiple OR conditions?
modules.moduleProject: Create a search-and-cleanup worksheet for a customer table with missing or inconsistent information.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Create a search-and-cleanup worksheet for a customer table with missing or inconsistent information.

sections.title: Text Matching
sections.description: Search text with LIKE and wildcards so queries can handle partial matches and flexible patterns.

sections.title: Lists and Ranges
sections.description: Use IN, NOT IN, and BETWEEN to write cleaner filters for lists and ranges.

sections.title: Missing Data
sections.description: Understand NULL and learn the right way to find missing or present values.

sections.title: Practice
sections.description: Combine text matching, list filters, ranges, and NULL checks in practical search problems.

modules.description: Expand filtering to real-world cases like partial text, multiple choices, ranges, and missing values.

modules.title: Calculated Columns and SQL Expressions
modules.purpose: Teach learners to create new values from existing data so raw tables become useful reports.
learningObjectives: Use arithmetic and expressions inside SELECT.
Rename outputs with aliases for readability.
Apply simple built-in functions safely.
guidedExercises: Calculate line totals from price and quantity.
Create a discounted price column.
Use aliases to make a result set presentation-ready.
quizFocus: What is an expression in SQL?
Why are aliases useful in reports?
What happens when an expression uses NULL values?
modules.moduleProject: Build a simple invoice or payroll report with calculated columns and clear output names.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Build a simple invoice or payroll report with calculated columns and clear output names.

sections.title: Creating New Values in Queries
sections.description: Create useful calculated values directly inside SELECT statements using arithmetic and expressions.

sections.title: Column Aliases
sections.description: Rename calculated and selected columns so query results are easier to read and explain.

sections.title: Simple Functions
sections.description: Explore beginner-friendly SQL functions for text, numbers, and dates without overcomplicating the query.

sections.title: Expression Practice
sections.description: Use expressions and aliases to build simple report-style outputs such as totals, discounts, and renamed columns.

modules.description: Teach learners to create new values from existing data so raw tables become useful reports.

modules.title: Aggregate Functions
modules.purpose: Move from row-by-row reading to summarizing many rows into a single business answer.
learningObjectives: Use core aggregate functions correctly.
Summarize filtered subsets of data.
Interpret aggregate output in business terms.
guidedExercises: Count the number of customers in a table.
Find total revenue for a filtered set of orders.
Calculate average score or salary for a chosen group of rows.
quizFocus: What is the difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(column)?
Why are aggregates useful for dashboards and reporting?
How does WHERE interact with an aggregate query?
modules.moduleProject: Create a one-page KPI report for a tiny business dataset using only aggregate queries.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Create a one-page KPI report for a tiny business dataset using only aggregate queries.

sections.title: Intro to Aggregation
sections.description: Move from individual rows to summary answers by learning what aggregate functions do.

sections.title: Core Aggregate Functions
sections.description: Practice COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, and MAX as the core tools for summarizing data.

sections.title: Aggregates with Filters
sections.description: Combine WHERE with aggregates to summarize only the rows that matter.

sections.title: Aggregate Practice
sections.description: Build small summary reports using counts, totals, averages, highest values, and lowest values.

modules.description: Move from row-by-row reading to summarizing many rows into a single business answer.

modules.title: Grouping Data with GROUP BY and HAVING
modules.purpose: Teach learners to summarize by category, person, team, or product instead of only summarizing the whole table.
learningObjectives: Write grouped summaries with GROUP BY.
Choose between WHERE and HAVING based on the stage of filtering.
Explain grouped result sets clearly.
guidedExercises: Count orders by customer.
Find average salary by department.
Show only groups that exceed a chosen threshold using HAVING.
quizFocus: Why can't HAVING fully replace WHERE?
What happens when a selected column is neither grouped nor aggregated?
Why does GROUP BY make result sets shorter than the original table?
modules.moduleProject: Create a management summary report with grouped counts, sums, and averages.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Create a management summary report with grouped counts, sums, and averages.

sections.title: Intro to Grouping
sections.description: Learn how GROUP BY turns many rows into summaries by category, person, product, or team.

sections.title: Aggregates with Groups
sections.description: Use aggregate functions with groups to calculate counts, totals, averages, minimums, and maximums per category.

sections.title: Filtering Groups
sections.description: Understand HAVING and how it filters grouped summaries differently from WHERE.

sections.title: Grouping Practice
sections.description: Create grouped reports for sales, orders, departments, and dashboard-style summaries.

modules.description: Teach learners to summarize by category, person, team, or product instead of only summarizing the whole table.

modules.title: Introduction to Joins
modules.purpose: Show how relational databases connect information stored in different tables.
learningObjectives: Explain why related data is split across tables.
Write INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN queries.
Identify missing matches and interpret NULLs after joins.
guidedExercises: Join customers to orders and show a combined report.
Use LEFT JOIN to find rows that do not have a match.
Sketch the relationship before writing the query.
quizFocus: What is the difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN?
Why are keys important in join logic?
Why do NULL values appear in some left-joined results?
modules.moduleProject: Build a multi-table report that combines at least three tables from a school, store, or HR dataset.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Build a multi-table report that combines at least three tables from a school, store, or HR dataset.

sections.title: Why Multiple Tables Exist
sections.description: Understand why databases split related information across tables and how IDs connect that data.

sections.title: INNER JOIN
sections.description: Learn INNER JOIN by matching related rows from two tables and reading the combined result.

sections.title: LEFT JOIN
sections.description: Use LEFT JOIN to keep unmatched rows and understand why NULL appears in joined results.

sections.title: Join Practice
sections.description: Practice choosing the right join for common relationships like customers and orders, students and courses, and products and categories.

modules.description: Show how relational databases connect information stored in different tables.

modules.title: Writing Cleaner Queries
modules.purpose: Improve readability so beginners do not just write working SQL, but SQL they can revisit, debug, and teach from.
learningObjectives: Use aliases and formatting to improve clarity.
Write SQL that is easy to debug and review.
Add comments only when they genuinely help understanding.
guidedExercises: Reformat three one-line queries into readable SQL.
Rename poor aliases to clearer ones.
Add concise comments to a query with non-obvious logic.
quizFocus: When are table aliases helpful versus unnecessary?
Why should SQL be broken across lines?
What makes a query easy for another person to maintain?
modules.moduleProject: Take a messy starter script and refactor it into a clean, readable reporting query pack.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Take a messy starter script and refactor it into a clean, readable reporting query pack.

sections.title: Aliases for Tables and Columns
sections.description: Use table and column aliases to make longer queries shorter, clearer, and easier to maintain.

sections.title: Query Formatting
sections.description: Format SQL across lines with indentation so the structure of the query is easy to follow.

sections.title: Comments and Readability
sections.description: Add helpful comments and readable names without cluttering queries or hiding the logic.

sections.title: Clean Query Practice
sections.description: Refactor messy SQL into clean query examples that are easier to debug, review, and explain.

modules.description: Improve readability so beginners do not just write working SQL, but SQL they can revisit, debug, and teach from.

modules.title: Subqueries for Beginners
modules.purpose: Introduce query-inside-query thinking without overwhelming learners with advanced database theory.
learningObjectives: Read subqueries from the inside out.
Use simple subqueries in WHERE and FROM.
Recognize when a join might be clearer than a subquery.
guidedExercises: Find products priced above the overall average.
Use IN with a subquery to find customers who placed orders.
Wrap a grouped query and filter it in an outer query.
quizFocus: Why are aliases important for subqueries in FROM?
What does it mean to read a subquery inside out?
When might a join be easier to understand?
modules.moduleProject: Build a comparison worksheet showing two solutions to the same problem: one with a join and one with a subquery.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Build a comparison worksheet showing two solutions to the same problem: one with a join and one with a subquery.

sections.title: What a Subquery Is
sections.description: Introduce subqueries as queries inside other queries and learn when that pattern is useful.

sections.title: Subqueries in WHERE
sections.description: Use subqueries inside WHERE to compare rows against calculated values or lists of results.

sections.title: Subqueries in FROM

topics.title: Build on top of another query

sections.description: Treat a subquery as a temporary result set that another query can build on.
bullets: Build on top of another query

sections.title: Subquery Practice
sections.description: Practice solving beginner problems with subqueries and compare them with join-based thinking.

modules.description: Introduce query-inside-query thinking without overwhelming learners with advanced database theory.

modules.title: Inserting Data
modules.purpose: Shift from reading data to adding new rows carefully and correctly.
learningObjectives: Insert new rows with confidence.
Use explicit column lists for safer statements.
Recognize how defaults and NULL values affect inserts.
guidedExercises: Insert one valid row into a table.
Insert multiple rows in a single statement.
Check the table afterward to confirm what changed.
quizFocus: Why is it safer to name columns in INSERT statements?
What happens if a required column is missing?
When can NULL be inserted intentionally?
modules.moduleProject: Create a seed-data script that loads starter data for a simple student, product, or book database.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Create a seed-data script that loads starter data for a simple student, product, or book database.

sections.title: Intro to Adding Data
sections.description: Learn what INSERT INTO does and how SQL adds new rows to a table.

sections.title: Safe Inserting
sections.description: Use explicit column lists and type awareness to make insert statements safer and easier to debug.

sections.title: Insert with NULL and Defaults
sections.description: Understand how missing values, NULL, optional columns, and defaults affect inserted rows.

sections.title: Insert Practice
sections.description: Practice adding students, products, and multiple records, then checking the inserted data.

modules.description: Shift from reading data to adding new rows carefully and correctly.

modules.title: Updating and Deleting Data
modules.purpose: Teach safe data changes and the habit of previewing before modifying records.
learningObjectives: Update and delete rows intentionally.
Preview affected rows before running changes.
Adopt safe habits that prevent accidental data loss.
guidedExercises: Run a SELECT preview for rows you plan to update.
Update a filtered subset of rows.
Delete only temporary or test rows after confirming the target set.
quizFocus: Why should UPDATE and DELETE almost always be paired with WHERE?
What is a safe preview workflow before data changes?
Why is accidental deletion more dangerous than a bad SELECT query?
modules.moduleProject: Write a maintenance script that updates statuses, fixes one field, and removes obsolete test data using safe preview steps.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Write a maintenance script that updates statuses, fixes one field, and removes obsolete test data using safe preview steps.

sections.title: Updating Rows
sections.description: Learn how UPDATE changes existing rows and why WHERE is critical before making changes.

sections.title: Deleting Rows
sections.description: Use DELETE carefully by selecting target rows first and avoiding accidental full-table deletion.

sections.title: Data Safety Mindset
sections.description: Build safe habits for previewing, backing up, and thinking before changing data.

sections.title: Practice with Data Changes
sections.description: Practice updating prices, changing statuses, deleting test rows, and following a safe modification workflow.

modules.description: Teach safe data changes and the habit of previewing before modifying records.

modules.title: Creating Tables and Database Structure
modules.purpose: Introduce schema design so learners can build small databases instead of only querying prebuilt ones.
learningObjectives: Create basic relational tables with sensible data types.
Use core constraints to protect data quality.
Explain why schema choices affect future queries.
guidedExercises: Design a students table and justify each column.
Create a products table with a primary key and price column.
Link two tables with a foreign key.
quizFocus: What does a primary key guarantee?
Why is NOT NULL useful?
How does a foreign key support relational design?
modules.moduleProject: Design and create the full schema for a tiny business or school database with at least three related tables.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Design and create the full schema for a tiny business or school database with at least three related tables.

sections.title: Intro to Table Design
sections.description: Learn what CREATE TABLE does and how clear column choices shape the future database.

sections.title: Common Data Types
sections.description: Choose beginner-friendly data types for integers, text, decimals, dates, and times.

sections.title: Constraints
sections.description: Use constraints like PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, and FOREIGN KEY to protect data quality.

sections.title: Table Design Practice
sections.description: Design and create simple tables, then connect them with keys in a small relational structure.

modules.description: Introduce schema design so learners can build small databases instead of only querying prebuilt ones.

modules.title: Relational Database Design for Beginners
modules.purpose: Strengthen the modeling side of SQL so learners understand why tables are shaped the way they are.
learningObjectives: Recognize common relationship patterns.
Reduce duplication by separating repeated information.
Turn a real-world scenario into related tables.
guidedExercises: Identify relationship types in three short scenarios.
Redesign a denormalized table into multiple related tables.
Draw a basic entity-relationship sketch before writing SQL.
quizFocus: What is the difference between one-to-many and many-to-many?
Why is duplicate data dangerous?
Why should tables stay focused on one main subject?
modules.moduleProject: Create a beginner ERD and matching CREATE TABLE script for a chosen domain such as school, bookstore, clinic, or art store.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Create a beginner ERD and matching CREATE TABLE script for a chosen domain such as school, bookstore, clinic, or art store.

sections.title: Why Relationships Matter
sections.description: Recognize how real-world relationships become one-to-one, one-to-many, and many-to-many database patterns.

sections.title: Keys and Relationships
sections.description: Review primary and foreign keys as the foundation for linking tables properly.

sections.title: Good Beginner Design Habits
sections.description: Build better table designs by reducing duplication, keeping tables focused, and planning for future queries.

sections.title: Relational Design Practice
sections.description: Turn real scenarios like stores, enrollments, and orders into beginner-friendly relational designs.

modules.description: Strengthen the modeling side of SQL so learners understand why tables are shaped the way they are.

modules.title: Real Beginner SQL Projects and Course Capstone
modules.purpose: Consolidate everything into realistic beginner projects that require reading, filtering, grouping, joining, and basic data design.
learningObjectives: Combine multiple beginner SQL skills in a single workflow.
Plan, build, query, and explain a small relational database.
Finish the course with portfolio-ready beginner projects.
guidedExercises: Choose one domain and create starter tables plus sample data.
Answer at least ten business questions with SQL.
Present the final project as a short written report with queries and explanations.
quizFocus: Which SQL skills appear most often in real reports?
How do you decide what tables and columns a project needs?
Why is being able to explain a query just as important as writing it?
modules.moduleProject: Complete a capstone database project with schema, seed data, 10-15 queries, one grouped report, one multi-table join report, and a brief written explanation.
modules.recommendedPacing: 2-4 lessons depending on depth, assignments, and live practice time.
modules.typicalOutcome: Complete a capstone database project with schema, seed data, 10-15 queries, one grouped report, one multi-table join report, and a brief written explanation.

sections.title: Mini Project: Student Database
sections.description: Plan a small student database, insert sample data, and answer useful questions with SQL.

sections.title: Mini Project: Store Database
sections.description: Design a store database with products, customers, orders, and sales queries.

sections.title: Mini Project: Employee Database
sections.description: Create employee and department reports using salary analysis, summaries, and joined data.

sections.title: Capstone Practice
sections.description: Bring the full course together by asking business questions, writing complete SQL solutions, and explaining the logic.

modules.description: Consolidate everything into realistic beginner projects that require reading, filtering, grouping, joining, and basic data design.

assessmentAndDelivery.suggestedBeginnerRhythm: teach -> model one example -> guided practice -> short verbal check -> mini independent exercise.
recommendedCourseDeliverables: A short quiz at the end of most modules.
A practice sheet with 5-10 queries after each technical module.
At least 3 mini projects before the final capstone.
A final portfolio-style capstone in Module 15.

selfPacedTrack: Complete 1 module per week for a 16-week path.
Spend extra practice time after Modules 2, 7, 8, and 13.
Rebuild your capstone from scratch after finishing the course.

toolingSuggestions: Start with a browser-based SQL sandbox or SQLite environment.
Use small, familiar datasets before moving into larger multi-table examples.
Encourage learners to read every query result out loud in plain English.
Save one clean query notebook or cheat sheet per module.
assessmentAndDelivery.closingNote: Absolute beginners do best when the course avoids jumping too early into joins, subqueries, or theory-heavy design. This sequence deliberately builds confidence first, then complexity.

moduleMilestones.learnersCan: Understand tables and write simple SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY, and LIMIT queries.
Handle text and NULL values, create calculated columns, summarize data, and group results.
Read across multiple tables and solve slightly more advanced problems.
Insert, update, delete, create tables, and model beginner relational systems.
Plan and execute a small end-to-end SQL project.
